coredata.dc:title: Effectively enhanced photoluminescence of CePO4:Tb3+ nanorods combined with carbon dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 Chinese Society of Rare Earths. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Chinese Society of Rare Earths. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Yanyan
Yue, Dan
Tian, Boshi
Li, Weidong
Zhang, Bing
Yang, Liu
Li, Yue
Wang, Zhenling
Zhang, Yilei
coredata.dc:description: CePO4:Tb3+ nanorods were successfully obtained via a simple hydrothermal method and combined with carbon dots (CDs) to obtain CDs@CePO4:Tb3+ nanorods. Due to the combination of CDs, the emission intensity of CDs@CePO4:Tb3+ nanorods increases about 92 times, compared with that of CePO4:Tb3+ nanorods. The combination of CDs and CePO4:Tb3+ nanorods was confirmed by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and so on. The mechanism of luminescence enhancement may be attributed to some aspects: the formation of hexagonal phase results in the increase of crystal field symmetry, and the energy transfer among CDs, Ce3+ and Tb3+ ions, which causes the Tb3+ ions in CDs@CePO4:Tb3+ nanorods to obtain more excited energy and less non-radiative attenuation compared to CePO4: Tb3+ nanorods. The luminescence enhancement strategy through combination of CDs would provide a simple and effective approach for other rare earth ions doped luminescent materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CePO4
Rare earth ions
Doping
Carbon dots
Photoluminescence enhancement

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Luminescent properties of CePO4:Tb3+ and CDs@CePO4:Tb3+ nanorods
The role of CDs in luminescence enhancement

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD patterns of CePO4:Tb3+ nanorods and CDs@CePO4:Tb3+ composites synthesized with different amounts of CDs solution; (b) Local magnified XRD patterns in the range of 15°–35° marked by square frame in (a).
FTIR spectra of CePO4: Tb3+ (black line) and CDs@CePO4: Tb3+ (red line) nanorods.
TG curves of CePO4:Tb3+ (black line) and CDs@CePO4:Tb3+ (red line) nanorods.
TEM and HRTEM images of CePO4:Tb3+ (a, b) and CDs@CePO4:Tb3+ (c, d) nanorods.
Emission spectra of CDs@CePO4: Tb3+ nanorods synthesized with different amounts of CDs solution (0, 0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7, 0.9, 1.5, 2.0, 2.5, 3.0 and 3.5 mL).
Luminescent decay curves of CePO4:Tb3+ (black block) and CDs@CePO4:Tb3+ (red dot) nanorods.
UV–vis spectra of CePO4:Tb3+ (black line) and CDs@CePO4:Tb3+ (red line) nanorods.
High-resolution XPS spectra of CDs@CePO4:Tb3+ nanorods. (a) C 1s; (b) O 1s.
Schematic energy-level and luminescence diagram of CDs@CePO4:Tb3+ nanorods.

ce:article-footnote.ce:note-para: Foundation item: Project supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51572303, 52002117).

head.ce:title: Effectively enhanced photoluminescence of CePO4:Tb3+ nanorods combined with carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Henan International Joint Laboratory of Rare Earth Composite Materials, College of Materials Engineering, Henan Key Laboratory of Electronic Ceramic Materials and Application, Henan University of Engineering, Xinzheng 451191, China

Henan Key Laboratory of Electronic Ceramic Materials and Application

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Henan Key Laboratory of Rare Earth Functional Materials, Zhoukou Normal University, Zhoukou 466001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Henan Key Laboratory of Rare Earth Functional Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: . Henan Key Laboratory of Rare Earth Functional Materials, Zhoukou Normal University, Zhoukou, 466001, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Civil & Mechanical Engineering, University of Canterbury, Christchurch, 8140, New Zealand
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: . Civil & Mechanical Engineering, University of Canterbury, Christchurch, 8140, New Zealand

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: CePO4:Tb3+ nanorods were successfully obtained via a simple hydrothermal method and combined with carbon dots (CDs) to obtain CDs@CePO4:Tb3+ nanorods. Due to the combination of CDs, the emission intensity of CDs@CePO4:Tb3+ nanorods increases about 92 times, compared with that of CePO4:Tb3+ nanorods. The combination of CDs and CePO4:Tb3+ nanorods was confirmed by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and so on. The mechanism of luminescence enhancement may be attributed to some aspects: the formation of hexagonal phase results in the increase of crystal field symmetry, and the energy transfer among CDs, Ce3+ and Tb3+ ions, which causes the Tb3+ ions in CDs@CePO4:Tb3+ nanorods to obtain more excited energy and less non-radiative attenuation compared to CePO4: Tb3+ nanorods. The luminescence enhancement strategy through combination of CDs would provide a simple and effective approach for other rare earth ions doped luminescent materials.

An effective strategy was developed to enhance luminescence through combining carbon dots to form CDs@CePO4:Tb3+ composites, and the emission intensity increases 92 times.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Rare earth ions doped lanthanide phosphate (LnPO4) is a kind of promising phosphor, due to its high photostability and chemical stability, long photoluminescence lifetime, low cytotoxicity, narrow launch bandwidth and enduring high temperature without decomposition, which promotes the development of rare earth ions doped lanthanide phosphate in color display, solid state lighting, biomedical application, etc. 1–5 In recent years, many researchers have devoted to the synthesis and application of rare earth ions doped lanthanide phosphates. For example, Ruan et al. 6 reported that the well-dispersed TbPO4: Eu3+ nanocrystals were synthesized with oleic acid as surfactant, and multicolor emissions from green to red were achieved by changing the doping concentration of Eu3+ ions. The broadened spectral selectivity and reduced interference with biological systems make these TbPO4: Eu3+ nanocrystals be better for biomarkers. GdPO4·H2O hydrogel was prepared with GdCl3 and KH2PO4 at low temperature and could be used for encapsulation and drug release, and as scaffolds to promote cell proliferation. 7 Sharma et al. 8 prepared orange-red emitting SmPO4 phosphors at high temperature, and these materials have ultra-high thermal stability under near-ultraviolet excitation, which promotes its application in solid state lighting devices. Zeng et al. proposed the one-step optical strategy for fast, sensitive identification of alkaline phosphatase (ALP) by utilizing the significant fluorescence differences between P2O7:Ce3+,Tb3+ and PO4:Ce3+,Tb3+. The pyrophosphate (PPi) combined with the rare earth ions Ce3+, Tb3+ to generate P2O7:Ce3+,Tb3+ nanoparticles, which exhibit the bright green emission. While in the presence of ALP, the enzyme substrate PPi was broken down into PO4 3−, which can self-assemble with Ce3+, Tb3+ ions to obtain PO4:Ce3+,Tb3+ nanoparticles, exhibiting the weak green light. 9

Ce3+ is a highly effective sensitizer, which can transfer excited energy to Tb3+ ions and enhance the green luminescence of Tb3+ ions. 10 Therefore, the research on CePO4:Tb3+ has attracted wide attention in recent years. 11 Chen et al. synthesized CePO4:Tb3+ nanotube through solvothermal method, and found that these samples could be reversibly switched by Ce4+/Ce3+ redox coupling and used as redox switch for biomarkers and fluorescent probes. 12 It has been confirmed that CePO4:Tb3+ colloidal solution had a linear response to H2O2 in the concentration range of 0–200 μmol/L, and could be used as fluorescent probes for rapid quantitative detection of H2O2. 13 Single phase white light CePO4:Tb3+,Sm3+ nanocrystals were synthesized using sol–gel method, and the CIE chromaticity coordinates, color temperature and high color rendering index suggested that CePO4:Tb3+,Sm3+ nanocrystals could be potential candidates in the field of white light applications. 14

The above investigation and applications put forward higher requirements on the luminescence performance of CePO4:Tb3+ nanomaterials. Most of researchers improved the luminescence intensity of CePO4:Tb3+ through forming core–shell structure. For example, Li et al. 15 reported that the luminescence efficiency of CePO4:Tb3+/LaPO4 core–shell nanowires has been improved about 7 times due to the addition of LaPO4 coating. Two kinds of hexagonal CePO4:Tb3+/GdPO4 and GdPO4/CePO4:Tb3+ core–shell structures were synthesized through ultrasonic assisted method, compared with CePO4:Tb3+, the fluorescence intensity increased about 33 times and 7 times, respectively. 16 Yang et al. 17 synthesized core-shell-shell SiO2@CePO4:Tb3+@SiO2 submicro-spheres by connecting rare earth ions of silica core and bridging organosilicon ligands, and used the protection of SiO2 shell to improve its luminescence performance. A few researchers improved the luminescence performance of CePO4:Tb3+ through addition of silica sols. 18 Besides, CePO4:Tb3+ nanocrystals capped with tributylphosphate (TBP) showed enhanced emission intensity compared with the uncapped ones. 19 However, these methods have more reactive steps and fewer enhanced multiples. Therefore, it is still worth finding a simple and effective method to improve the luminescence performance of CePO4:Tb3+ nanomaterials.

Carbon dots (CDs) are a new type of fluorescence materials with size smaller than 10 nm, and have excellent optical properties, low toxicity, high sensitivity, tunable fluorescence emission and excitation, good photostability and biocompatibility. 20 Hence, fluorescence CDs are promising in the field of fluorescent display, biochemical detection, light emitting diode, photocatalysis, solar cell and so on. 21
,
22 For example, Hui et al. 23 stabilized perovskite solar cells through combining red-carbon quantum dots (RCQs) with SnO2 treated with cryogenic solution. The carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on the surface of RCQs can interact with uncoordinated ions on the interface, reducing ion migration and nonradiative recombination, which conduce to the reduction of hysteresis of perovskite solar cells to maintain long-term stability. B4C-BGQD composites were synthesized and modified with B-doped graphene quantum dots (BGQD), which has good catalytic activity for electrochemical reduction of N2 to NH3 at low over-potential. This is because BGQD has a large number of exposed sites accelerating the charge transfer in the process of N2 reduction. 24 Graphene quantum dots dispersed in conjugated polymers provided more electrical transport paths, which brings about an enhancement of charge injection and increasement of carrier density, thus improving the organic photovoltaic characteristics significantly. 25 N-TiO2/C-dots composite displayed enhanced electrical conductivity and charge transfer properties, because of the N species on the surface of carbon dots reducing the band gap. 26 To the best of our knowledge, there are almost no reports on the introduction of CDs to improve the luminescent performance of CePO4:Tb3+ phosphors. In this work, we confirmed the effective combination of CDs and CePO4:Tb3+ nanorods and the prominent luminescence enhancement of CePO4:Tb3+ nanorods. Compared to CePO4:Tb3+ nanorods without CDs, the luminescence intensity of CDs@CePO4:Tb3+ nanorods increases about 92 times. The mechanism of luminescence enhancement was discussed and this investigation might provide a simple and effective strategy to enhance luminescence performance for other rare earth ions doped luminescent materials.

Ce(NO3)3·6H2O (analytical reagent, AR, 99.99%), Tb(NO3)3·6H2O (AR, 99.99%), NH4H2PO4 (AR, 99%), 1,2,4,5-benzenetetracarboxylic acid (AR, 98%) and N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) were purchased from Shanghai Sa En Chemical Technology Co., Ltd. All chemicals were used without further purification.

The synthetic method of CePO4: Tb3+ nanorods is similar to that in the literature. 27
,
28 Typically, 5.7 mmol of Ce(NO3)3·6H2O and 0.3 mmol of Tb(NO3)3·6H2O were added into 60.0 mL of distilled water under continuously stirring for 15 min. Then 60.0 mL of distilled water dissolved 12.0 mmol of NH4H2PO4 was quickly poured into the above solution under stirring for 5 min to get the white mixing solution. After that, the above mixture was sealed in a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 170 °C for 10 h. After the autoclave was cooled to room temperature, the precipitation was separated by centrifugation, washed with distilled water and absolute ethanol for several times. Finally, CePO4:5 mol%Tb3+ nanorods were obtained after dried at 80 °C for 12 h.

CDs were prepared according to the previous reported method, using 1,2,4,5-benzenetetracarboxylic acid and DMF as the starting materials. 29 The preparation of CDs@CePO4:Tb3+ nanorods is similar to that of CePO4:Tb3+ nanorods, except that the different quantities of CDs were added to the white mixing solution and stirred for a period of time before transferred to the 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. The other synthesis procedure was similar to the synthesis of CePO4:5 mol%Tb3+ nanorods and the CDs@CePO4:Tb3+ nanorods were obtained after dried at 80 °C for 12 h.

The D8 Advance X-ray powder diffractometer (XRD) produced by Germany Bruker Company was used to analyze the phase of the sample, using Cu target as radiant source (λ = 0.154056 nm, tube voltage: 40 kV, tube current: 40 mA). The fluorescence spectra and lifetime of the samples were measured by the FLS920P fluorescent test system produced by Edinburgh Company, with 450 W xenon lamp and μF900H microsecond flash lamp as the excitation light sources. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was carried out within KBr slices in the 4000–400 cm−1 range using a Nexus 912 AO446 infrared spectrum radiometer. Raman spectral analysis was performed using a Raman spectrometer (Renishaw, inVia Reflex) to determine the vibrational properties of CePO4: Tb3+ at an excitation wavelength of 523 nm in the scan range of 100–3200 cm−1. UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) were recorded at room temperature using a UV-3600 spectrophotometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscope (XPS) was obtained by a ThermoFischer ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, and all binding energies were referenced to the C 1s peak at 284.80 eV of surface amorphous carbon. The transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL 2010) was used to observe the morphology of the samples. Thermogravimetric analysis (TG) was performed on STA449 F3 produced by Netzsch Company and the heating rate was 10 K/min.

Fig. 1 (a) shows the XRD patterns of CePO4:Tb3+ and CDs@CePO4:Tb3+ nanorods. It can be seen that CePO4:Tb3+ nanorods without any CDs (CDs = 0 mL) are indexed as monoclinic phase by comparison with JCPDS card 84-0247 of CePO4 powders. The as-prepared CDs have amorphous structure, as determined by the XRD pattern of CDs (Fig. S1). When the different amounts of CDs were added into the reaction mixture during the process of preparing CePO4: Tb3+ nanorods, the XRD patterns of CDs@CePO4: Tb3+ nanorods gradually match well with the hexagonal phase data of CePO4 powders (JCPDS card 34-1380) with the increase of addition amount of CDs. As shown in Fig. 1(a, b), when no CDs were added (CDs = 0 mL), the peaks at 2θ = 19.0°, 21.3°, 27.0°, 28.9°, 31.3° and 34.6° are attributed to (011), (−111) (200), (120) (012) and (−202) plane of monoclinic CePO4, respectively. When 0.5 mL of CDs were added, the peaks at 2θ = 19.0°, 21.3°, 27.0° and 34.6° become very weak. And these peaks completely disappear when the addition amount of CDs is 1, 3, 5, 8 mL. Meanwhile, the peaks at 2θ = 20.0°, 25.2°, 28.8°, 29.2° and 31.4° are present, which can be indexed to (101), (110) (111), (200) and (102) planes of hexagonal CePO4, respectively. This indicates that the use of CDs is beneficial to the synthesis of CDs@CePO4:Tb3+ composites with hexagonal phase.

In order to confirm the formation of CDs@CePO4:Tb3+ composites, a series of characterizations including FTIR, Raman spectrum, TEM and TG analysis were used to investigate the existence of CDs in the CDs@CePO4:Tb3+ nanorods. It can be seen from the FTIR spectra (Fig. 2 ) that there are significant differences between CePO4:Tb3+ and CDs@CePO4:Tb3+ nanorods. As for CePO4:Tb3+ nanorods (black line), the absorption peaks are observed at 614, 1047, 1384 and 1628 cm−1, which are attributed to O=P–O bending, P–O stretching, –COO stretching and C=O stretching vibration, respectively. Besides, the absorption band around 3460 cm−1 is assigned to –OH stretching. 30
,
31 As for CDs@CePO4:Tb3+ nanorods (red line), the absorption peaks O=P–O bending and P–O stretching are similar to CePO4:Tb3+ nanorods, but the intensity of –COO stretching and C=O stretching vibration are much stronger due to the combination of CDs. The surface of the CDs contain abundant hydroxyl, carboxyl and carbonyl groups, hence the combination of CDs makes the peaks of –OH stretching, –COO stretching and C=O stretching vibration stronger. For O=P–O bending vibration, more dissociative PO4 3− are probably gained due to the formation of coordination bonds between Ce3+ ions and carboxyl oxygen atoms in CDs. 32
,
33 Based on above discussion, it is concluded that CDs have been successfully combined with CePO4:Tb3+ nanorods.

Fig. S2 shows the Raman spectra of CePO4: Tb3+ and CDs@CePO4: Tb3+ nanorods. It can be seen that the Raman peaks of CDs@CePO4: Tb3+ nanorods split into three small peaks around 1800 cm−1, and a new small peak appears at 2940 cm−1 after adding CDs. The three small peaks around 1800 cm−1 are assigned to C=N stretching, C=C stretching, and C=O stretching vibration respectively, and the weak peak at 2940 cm−1 in Raman spectra might be attributed to C–H vibration mode associated with unsaturated bonds. 34 It is known that CDs contain a large number of unsaturated bonds such as C=O, C=N, and have abundant groups containing H atoms such as –OH, –COOH, therefore, the peak splitting and appearance of new weak peak also confirm the successful recombination of CDs to some extent. This is consistent with the results obtained by FTIR analysis.

The TG curves of CePO4:Tb3+ and CDs@CePO4:Tb3+ nanorods are shown in Fig. 3 . It can be seen that the TG curve of CDs@CePO4: Tb3+ nanorods exhibits three mass losses within the temperature range of 100–800 °C, with a total weightlessness of 8.4%. The first is about 0.9% weight loss in the temperature range of 100–155 °C, which is attributed to the loss of a small amount of absorbed water. The second weight loss (5.2%) is observed at 155–221 °C, due to the thermal decomposition of the organic functional groups adsorbed on the sample. 35 The third weight loss is about 2.3% when the temperature is above 221 °C, and the curve gradually flattens out, this stage is generally attributed to further fracture of the C–C and C–H bonds. 35 As a comparison, the TG curve of CePO4: Tb3+ nanorods has only one weight loss trend in the 100–800 °C, and the total weight loss is about 5.5%, which is significantly different from the curve after combined with CDs.

To investigate the microstructure of CePO4:Tb3+ and CDs@CePO4:Tb3+ nanorods, the representative TEM and HRTEM images of CePO4:Tb3+ and CDs@CePO4:Tb3+ nanorods are given in Fig. 4 . It can be seen that both of them have rod-like morphology, and the CDs combining has little impact on the morphology (Fig. 4(a, c)). From HRTEM image of CePO4:Tb3+ nanorods (Fig. 4(b)), the well-resolved lattice fringes are clearly seen in Fig. 4(b), and the interplanar distance between adjacent lattice fringes is about 0.35 nm, corresponding to the (111) plane of the monoclinic CePO4. 36 As for CDs@CePO4:Tb3+ nanorods, the interplanar distance between adjacent lattice fringes is estimated to be 0.60 nm, agreeing well with the (100) plane of the hexagonal CePO4, 37 as shown in Fig. 4(d). These results are consistent with XRD analysis (Fig. 1). In addition, the other crystal plane spacing of 0.32 nm in Fig. 4(d) is indexed to d-spacing value of (002) plane of sp 2 graphitic carbon (PDF: 75-1621), 38 which confirms the presence of CDs in the CDs@CePO4:Tb3+ composites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Luminescent properties of CePO4:Tb3+ and CDs@CePO4:Tb3+ nanorods
ce:section.ce:para:
The photoluminescence (PL) spectra of CePO4:Tb3+ and CDs@CePO4:Tb3+ nanorods were used to determine whether the combination of CDs would affect the luminescence performance of CePO4:Tb3+ nanorods. The emission intensities of CePO4:Tb3+ nanorods (0 mL) and CDs@CePO4: Tb3+ nanorods with adding 0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7, 0.9, 1.5, 2.0, 2.5, 3.0, 3.5 mL of CDs solution were compared, as shown in Fig. 5 . The emission intensity (541 nm) of CDs@CePO4:Tb3+ nanorods increases about 1.2, 3, 84, 89 times when the addition amount of CDs is 0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7 mL, reaches the maximum (92 times) at 0.9 mL, and then the multiple gradually decreases with increasing CDs amount, as shown in Table S1. The higher adding amount of CDs above 0.9 mL probably leads to the increase of the quenching centers, which might affect the emission intensity of CePO4:Tb3+ nanorods to some degree. It can also be seen that the emission spectra show the characteristic emission peaks of doping Tb3+ ions, and the emission peaks at 488, 541, 586 and 620 nm are corresponding to 5D4-7F6, 5D4-7F5, 5D4-7F4 and 5D4-7F3 of Tb3+ ions, respectively. 39
Fig. S3 represents the PL excitation spectra of the CePO4: Tb3+ and CDs@CePO4:Tb3+ nanorods, and both of them exhibit a band around 262 nm monitored at 541 nm, attributed to the transition from the 4f ground state to the excited 5d state of Ce3+ ions. 19 However, the intensity of excitation spectrum of CDs@CePO4:Tb3+ nanorods is much stronger than that of CePO4:Tb3+ nanorods, which must have resulted from the combination of CDs. It is known that Ce3+ ions exhibit strong absorption in the range of 250–350 nm, which overlaps the emission band of CDs at 300–600 nm. 40 Therefore, the combination of CDs and CePO4:Tb3+ might result in effective energy transfer between CDs and Ce3+ ions.

In addition, the luminescent decay curves (Fig. 6 ) of CDs@CePO4:Tb3+ (λ em = 541 nm, λ ex = 262 nm, synthesized with 0.5 mL CDs solution) and CePO4:Tb3+ (λ em = 541 nm, λ ex = 262 nm) nanorods are well fitted into a double exponential function as shown in Eq. (1),
(1)
I = A 1 exp (-t/τ 1) + A 2 exp (-t/τ 2)
Where I is the emission intensity; τ 1 and τ 2 are the fast and slow components of the luminescent lifetime, respectively; A 1 and A 2 are the corresponding fitting parameters. The average lifetime can be determined by the formula according to Eq. (2),
(2)
τ = (A 1 τ 1 2+ A 2 τ 2 2) / (A 1 τ 1+ A 2 τ 2)
Through the fitting calculation, the average lifetimes of CDs@CePO4: Tb3+ and CePO4: Tb3+ nanorods are 3.86 and 3.68 ms, respectively.

The UV–vis spectra of CePO4:Tb3+ and CDs@CePO4:Tb3+ nanorods are shown in Fig. 7 . The UV–vis spectrum of CePO4:Tb3+ nanorods has five peaks at 212, 238, 256, 272 and 319 nm, corresponding to the transitions from the ground state 2F5/2 (4f 1 ) of Ce3+ ions to the five crystal field split levels of the Ce3+ ions 2D (5d 1 ) excited state. 37 The complete splitting into five energy levels of the Ce3+ ions 5d excited state also indicates that CePO4:Tb3+ nanorods have lower crystal field symmetry, 37 which is consistent with the result that CePO4:Tb3+ nanorods belong to monoclinic phase in XRD (Fig. 1). For CDs@CePO4:Tb3+ nanorods, the strongest peak at 272 nm might be assigning to the synergistic effect of the transition of Ce3+ ions and the π-π∗ transition of the aromatic C=C bond, and the broad band at 324 nm is classified into n-π∗ transitions of the C=O and C=N bonds. 41
,
42 It indicates that CDs are successfully combined and affect the crystal structure. After combination with CDs, the phase of CePO4:Tb3+ nanorods transfers from monoclinic to hexagonal phase and the crystal field symmetry is increased.

The XPS spectrum of CDs@CePO4:Tb3+ nanorods (Fig. S4) confirms the existence of Ce, O, C, Tb, and P elements. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of Ce 3d for CDs@CePO4:Tb3+ nanorods (Fig. S5) contains two main peaks with two shoulders. The two main peaks at 886.06 and 904.47 eV correspond to the spin–orbit split Ce (III) 3d5/2 and Ce 3d3/2 core holes, respectively. The two shoulders located at 882.42 and 900.73 eV are assigned to shake down satellites of Ce (III), probably involving O 2p → Ce (4f or 5d) charge transfer. 43 There are no characteristic peaks of Ce4+ state at its expected region (around 917 eV), indicating that the valence of cerium in the CDs@CePO4:Tb3+ nanorods is +3. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1s (Fig. 8 (a)) for CDs@CePO4:Tb3+ nanorods contains three peaks, the strong peak at 284.78 eV is assigned to C=C bond, and the weak peaks at 286.41 and 289.54 eV come from C–O (epoxy carbon or hydroxyl) and COOH respectively, further confirming the existence of CDs. 33
,
44 The high-resolution XPS spectrum of O 1s (Fig. 8(b)) for CDs@CePO4:Tb3+ nanorods has a single peak at 531.01 eV, which is ascribed to O–metal (bond to cerium atoms, Ce–O) bond. 44 CDs contain a large amount of carbonyl and carboxyl oxygen atoms, which might lead to the connection between CDs and Ce.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The role of CDs in luminescence enhancement
ce:section.ce:para: The role of CDs in luminescent enhancement of CDs@CePO4: Tb3+ nanorods probably lies in three aspects. The first is that the addition of CDs is beneficial to the synthesis of CDs@CePO4:Tb3+ composites with hexagonal phase. It has been reported that the luminescent properties of CePO4:Tb3+ nanorods depend on the crystal field symmetry determined by the phase structure to some extent. 45 After combination with CDs, the crystal field symmetry of CePO4:Tb3+ nanorods with hexagonal phase is higher than that of CePO4:Tb3+ nanorods with monoclinic. Similar to the results of Bao et al., 46 the hexagonal CDs@CePO4:Tb3+ nanorods with higher crystal field symmetry exhibit stronger photoluminescence properties than the monoclinic CePO4:Tb3+ nanorods.

The second is the energy transfer among CDs, Ce3+ and Tb3+ ions. The possible energy transfer processes are presented in Fig. 9 . In pure CePO4:Tb3+ system, Ce3+ ions are firstly excited by UV light, subsequently, energy transfer takes place from 5d (Ce3+) to the excitation high levels of Tb3+, followed by rapid internal conversion to the 5D4 level of Tb3+, which decays to various lower levels of 7F J (J = 0–6) to arise characteristic green emission of Tb3+ ions. 47 When CePO4:Tb3+ nanorods were combined with CDs, under the excitation of 262 nm UV light, besides Ce3+ ions are excited and transfer excited energy to Tb3+ ions, the ground state electrons of CDs are excited into the excited state as well. Due to the overlap of the CDs' emission band (300–600 nm) and Ce3+ ions’ absorption band (250–350 nm), the excited energy in CDs might transfer to the 5D3/2 or higher energy level of Ce3+ ions, thus more 5D3/2 excited state of Ce3+ ions could efficiently transfer energy to the 5D3 states of Tb3+ ions, non-radiatively decay to the 5D4 states and radiatively decay to various lower levels of 7F J (J = 0–6). The third might be attributed to the direct energy transfer from the excited energy in CDs to the 5D3 of Tb3+ ions. In a word, CDs@CePO4:Tb3+ nanorods obtain more energy and less non-radiative attenuation compared with that of CePO4:Tb3+ nanorods, resulting in the enhancement of green emission. 14
,
48

In conclusion, we developed a simple strategy to enhance the luminescence performance of CePO4:Tb3+ nanorods through combining with CDs. The results confirmed that the PL intensity of CDs@CePO4:Tb3+ nanorods has significantly increased about 92 times compared with CePO4:Tb3+ nanorods without combination of CDs. The possible mechanism of luminescence enhancement has some aspects: the formation of hexagonal phase results in the increase of crystal field symmetry, and the energy transfer from CDs to Ce3+ ions finally reaches Tb3+ ions, which leads to the Tb3+ ions in CDs@CePO4:Tb3+ nanorods obtaining more excited energy and less non-radiative attenuation compared to CePO4:Tb3+ nanorods. This research provides a simple and effective approach for enhancing the luminescence performance of rare earth ions doped phosphates.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jre.2021.10.006.
